title: HEY vs Fastmail for Minimalists
meta_description: HEY vs Fastmail for minimalists who want to control who can email them. See why HEY wins when you want to screen senders before messages reach your inbox.
constraint_lens: Minimalists prefer tools that remove ongoing inbox maintenance and avoid manual sorting steps.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for minimalists who want to approve senders before messages reach the inbox.
one_second_verdict.reason: Fastmail fails first because it delivers messages immediately and relies on manual filters after emails arrive.

verdict.summary: HEY is the better choice for minimalists who want strict control over who can email them. It forces every new sender through a screening step where the user must approve or block the address before the message enters the inbox. Fastmail accepts messages first and expects the user to manage unwanted senders using filters or manual cleanup afterward. That extra sorting work creates ongoing inbox maintenance that minimalists try to avoid.
verdict.decision_rule: If senders cannot be screened before reaching the inbox and must be filtered manually after arrival, Fastmail fails first.

sections.heading: Why HEY fits minimalists
sections.content: The user wants strict control over who can reach their inbox and refuses to manually unsubscribe from newsletters. HEY places all first time senders into a screening queue where the user must approve or reject them before any message appears in the inbox. Once rejected, the sender cannot contact the inbox again. This removes the need for filters or repeated unsubscribe actions.

bullets.point: HEY forces first time senders through a screening step before their message reaches the inbox.
bullets.why_it_matters: The user decides once whether a sender is allowed, preventing unwanted emails from ever entering the inbox.

bullets.point: HEY blocks rejected senders permanently after the screening decision.
bullets.why_it_matters: This mechanism stops repeat newsletters or promotional senders without requiring manual unsubscribe actions.

bullets.point: HEY separates newsletters into a dedicated reading stream instead of the main inbox.
bullets.why_it_matters: Marketing emails can be reviewed later without interrupting personal conversations.

bullets.point: Fastmail provides detailed filtering rules that move messages into folders based on sender or keywords.
bullets.why_it_matters: Users can automatically sort incoming mail into organized folders when managing larger inboxes.

bullets.point: Fastmail supports custom domains for personal or business email addresses.
bullets.why_it_matters: Users can host email under their own domain rather than using a shared provider address.

bullets.point: Fastmail offers a clean web interface without advertising panels.
bullets.why_it_matters: Users who want a simple email layout can read messages without promotional distractions.

items.fails_when: The user wants advanced rule based filtering and detailed folder automation for organizing complex inbox systems.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Fastmail where filtering rules can automatically move messages into folders.

items.fails_when: The user wants to block unwanted senders before messages arrive because Fastmail delivers emails immediately and relies on filters afterward.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use HEY where new senders must be approved before messages reach the inbox.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If the user prefers organizing incoming email using detailed filters and folders instead of screening senders individually, Fastmail may become the better option.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Pick HEY if you want to approve every sender before emails reach your inbox.
Pick HEY if you refuse to manually unsubscribe from newsletters.
Pick Fastmail if you prefer organizing email with filtering rules and folders.

faqs.a: HEY allows users to approve or block senders before their messages reach the inbox, preventing ongoing inbox cleanup.

faqs.q: Does Fastmail screen senders before email arrives?
faqs.a: No. Fastmail delivers messages immediately and relies on filters or manual actions afterward.

faqs.q: What is the HEY screening feature?
faqs.a: HEY places first time senders in a screening queue where users must approve or reject them before their messages reach the inbox.

faqs.q: When might Fastmail be a better choice?
faqs.a: Fastmail can be better for users who prefer organizing email with filters and folders rather than screening each sender.
